([/ˌfɪziˈɒlədʒi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language") [φύσις](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%86%CF%8D%CF%83%CE%B9%CF%82#Ancient_Greek "wikt:φύσις") *(*phúsis*)* 'nature, origin' and [-λογία](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%CE%BB%CE%BF%CE%B3%CE%AF%CE%B1#Ancient_Greek "wikt:-λογία") *(*[-logía](/wiki/-logy "-logy")*)* 'study of')[[1]](#cite_note-OnlineEtDict-1) is the [scientific](/wiki/Science "Science") study of [functions](/wiki/Function_(biology) "Function (biology)") and [mechanisms](/wiki/Mechanism_(biology) "Mechanism (biology)") in a [living system](/wiki/Life "Life").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Prosser-3) As a [subdiscipline](/wiki/Branches_of_science "Branches of science") of [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"), physiology focuses on how [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism"), [organ systems](/wiki/Organ_system "Organ system"), individual [organs](/wiki/Organ_(biology) "Organ (biology)"), [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)"), and [biomolecules](/wiki/Biomolecule "Biomolecule") carry out [chemical](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry") and [physical](/wiki/Physics "Physics") functions in a living system.[[4]](#cite_note-Guyton-4) According to the classes of [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism"), the field can be divided into [medical physiology](/wiki/Clinical_physiology "Clinical physiology"), [animal physiology](/wiki/Zoology#Physiology "Zoology"), [plant physiology](/wiki/Plant_physiology "Plant physiology"), [cell physiology](/wiki/Cell_physiology "Cell physiology"), and [comparative physiology](/wiki/Comparative_physiology "Comparative physiology").[[4]](#cite_note-Guyton-4)

**Physiology** ([/ˌfɪziˈɒlədʒi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language") [φύσις](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%86%CF%8D%CF%83%CE%B9%CF%82#Ancient_Greek "wikt:φύσις") *(*phúsis*)* 'nature, origin' and [-λογία](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%CE%BB%CE%BF%CE%B3%CE%AF%CE%B1#Ancient_Greek "wikt:-λογία") *(*[-logía](/wiki/-logy "-logy")*)* 'study of')[[1]](#cite_note-OnlineEtDict-1) is the [scientific](/wiki/Science "Science") study of [functions](/wiki/Function_(biology) "Function (biology)") and [mechanisms](/wiki/Mechanism_(biology) "Mechanism (biology)") in a [living system](/wiki/Life "Life").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Prosser-3) As a [subdiscipline](/wiki/Branches_of_science "Branches of science") of [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"), physiology focuses on how [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism"), [organ systems](/wiki/Organ_system "Organ system"), individual [organs](/wiki/Organ_(biology) "Organ (biology)"), [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)"), and [biomolecules](/wiki/Biomolecule "Biomolecule") carry out [chemical](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry") and [physical](/wiki/Physics "Physics") functions in a living system.[[4]](#cite_note-Guyton-4) According to the classes of [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism"), the field can be divided into [medical physiology](/wiki/Clinical_physiology "Clinical physiology"), [animal physiology](/wiki/Zoology#Physiology "Zoology"), [plant physiology](/wiki/Plant_physiology "Plant physiology"), [cell physiology](/wiki/Cell_physiology "Cell physiology"), and [comparative physiology](/wiki/Comparative_physiology "Comparative physiology").[[4]](#cite_note-Guyton-4)

Central to physiological functioning are [biophysical](/wiki/Biophysics "Biophysics") and [biochemical](/wiki/Biochemical "Biochemical") processes, [homeostatic](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis") control mechanisms, and [communication](/wiki/Cell_signaling "Cell signaling") between cells.[[5]](#cite_note-5) *Physiological state* is the condition of normal function. In contrast, *[pathological](/wiki/Pathology "Pathology") state* refers to [abnormal conditions](/wiki/Abnormality_(behavior) "Abnormality (behavior)"), including human [diseases](/wiki/Disease "Disease").

The [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") is awarded by the [Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Royal_Swedish_Academy_of_Sciences "Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences") for exceptional scientific achievements in physiology related to the field of [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine").

## Foundations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Foundations")]

Because physiology focuses on the functions and mechanisms of living organisms at all levels, from the molecular and cellular level to the level of whole organisms and populations, its foundations span a range of key disciplines:

* [Anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") is the study of the structure and organization of living organisms, from the microscopic level of cells and tissues to the macroscopic level of organs and systems. Anatomical knowledge is important in physiology because the structure and function of an organism are often dictated by one another.
* [Biochemistry](/wiki/Biochemistry "Biochemistry") is the study of the chemical processes and substances that occur within living organisms. Knowledge of biochemistry provides the foundation for understanding cellular and molecular processes that are essential to the functioning of organisms.
* [Biophysics](/wiki/Biophysics "Biophysics") is the study of the physical properties of living organisms and their interactions with their environment. It helps to explain how organisms sense and respond to different stimuli, such as light, sound, and temperature, and how they maintain homeostasis, or a stable internal environment.
* [Genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics") is the study of heredity and the variation of traits within and between populations. It provides insights into the genetic basis of physiological processes and the ways in which genes interact with the environment to influence an organism's phenotype.
* [Evolutionary biology](/wiki/Evolutionary_biology "Evolutionary biology") is the study of the processes that have led to the diversity of life on Earth. It helps to explain the origin and adaptive significance of physiological processes and the ways in which organisms have evolved to cope with their environment.

## Subdisciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Subdisciplines")]

There are many ways to categorize the subdisciplines of physiology:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

* based on the [taxa](/wiki/Taxon "Taxon") studied: [human physiology](/wiki/Human_physiology "Human physiology"), animal physiology, [plant physiology](/wiki/Plant_physiology "Plant physiology"), microbial physiology, viral physiology
* based on the [level of organization](/wiki/Biological_organisation "Biological organisation"): [cell physiology](/wiki/Cell_physiology "Cell physiology"), [molecular physiology](/wiki/Molecular_physiology "Molecular physiology"), [systems](/wiki/Biological_system "Biological system") physiology, organismal physiology, [ecological physiology](/wiki/Ecological_physiology "Ecological physiology"), integrative physiology
* based on the process that causes physiological variation: [developmental](/wiki/Developmental_biology "Developmental biology") physiology, [environmental physiology](/wiki/Environmental_physiology "Environmental physiology"), [evolutionary physiology](/wiki/Evolutionary_physiology "Evolutionary physiology")
* based on the ultimate goals of the research: [applied physiology](/wiki/Applied_physiology "Applied physiology") (e.g., medical physiology), [non-applied](/wiki/Fundamental_science "Fundamental science") (e.g., [comparative physiology](/wiki/Comparative_physiology "Comparative physiology"))

### Subdisciplines by level of organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Subdisciplines by level of organisation")]

#### Cell physiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cell physiology")]

Main article: [Cell physiology](/wiki/Cell_physiology "Cell physiology")

Although there are differences between [animal](/wiki/Animal "Animal"), [plant](/wiki/Plant "Plant"), and microbial cells, the basic physiological functions of cells can be divided into the processes of [cell division](/wiki/Cell_division "Cell division"), [cell signaling](/wiki/Cell_signaling "Cell signaling"), [cell growth](/wiki/Cell_growth "Cell growth"), and [cell metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Subdisciplines by taxa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Subdisciplines by taxa")]

#### Plant physiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Plant physiology")]

Main article: [Plant physiology](/wiki/Plant_physiology "Plant physiology")

Plant physiology is a subdiscipline of [botany](/wiki/Botany "Botany") concerned with the functioning of plants. Closely related fields include [plant morphology](/wiki/Plant_morphology "Plant morphology"), [plant ecology](/wiki/Plant_ecology "Plant ecology"), [phytochemistry](/wiki/Phytochemistry "Phytochemistry"), [cell biology](/wiki/Cell_biology "Cell biology"), [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"), [biophysics](/wiki/Biophysics "Biophysics"), and [molecular biology](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology"). Fundamental processes of [plant physiology](/wiki/Plant_physiology "Plant physiology") include [photosynthesis](/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis"), [respiration](/wiki/Respiration_in_plant "Respiration in plant"), [plant nutrition](/wiki/Plant_nutrition "Plant nutrition"), [tropisms](/wiki/Tropism "Tropism"), [nastic movements](/wiki/Nastic_movements "Nastic movements"), [photoperiodism](/wiki/Photoperiodism "Photoperiodism"), [photomorphogenesis](/wiki/Photomorphogenesis "Photomorphogenesis"), [circadian rhythms](/wiki/Circadian_rhythm "Circadian rhythm"), [seed germination](/wiki/Germination "Germination"), [dormancy](/wiki/Dormancy "Dormancy"), and [stomata](/wiki/Stoma "Stoma") function and [transpiration](/wiki/Transpiration "Transpiration"). Absorption of water by roots, production of food in the leaves, and growth of shoots towards light are examples of plant physiology.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

#### Animal physiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Animal physiology")]

Main article: [Biology § Animal form and function](/wiki/Biology#Animal_form_and_function "Biology")

#### Human physiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Human physiology")]

Main article: [Human body § Physiology](/wiki/Human_body#Physiology "Human body")

Human physiology is the study of how the human body's systems and functions work together to maintain a stable internal environment. It includes the study of the nervous, endocrine, cardiovascular, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems, as well as cellular and exercise physiology. Understanding human physiology is essential for diagnosing and treating health conditions and promoting overall wellbeing.

It seeks to understand the mechanisms that work to keep the [human body](/wiki/Human_body "Human body") alive and functioning,[[4]](#cite_note-Guyton-4) through scientific enquiry into the nature of mechanical, physical, and biochemical functions of humans, their organs, and the cells of which they are composed. The principal level of focus of physiology is at the level of organs and systems within systems. The endocrine and nervous systems play major roles in the reception and transmission of signals that integrate function in animals. [Homeostasis](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis") is a major aspect with regard to such interactions within plants as well as animals. The biological basis of the study of physiology, integration refers to the overlap of many functions of the systems of the human body, as well as its accompanied form. It is achieved through communication that occurs in a variety of ways, both electrical and chemical.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Changes in physiology can impact the mental functions of individuals. Examples of this would be the effects of certain medications or toxic levels of substances.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Change in [behavior](/wiki/Behavior_change_(individual) "Behavior change (individual)") as a result of these substances is often used to assess the health of individuals.[[10]](#cite_note-Davis2017-10)[[11]](#cite_note-zolDavis2017-11)

Much of the foundation of knowledge in human physiology was provided by [animal experimentation](/wiki/Animal_testing "Animal testing"). Due to the frequent connection between form and function, physiology and [anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") are intrinsically linked and are studied in tandem as part of a medical curriculum.[[12]](#cite_note-NIH2013-12)

### Subdisciplines by research objective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Subdisciplines by research objective")]

#### Comparative physiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Comparative physiology")]

Main article: [Comparative physiology](/wiki/Comparative_physiology "Comparative physiology")

Involving [evolutionary physiology](/wiki/Evolutionary_physiology "Evolutionary physiology") and [environmental physiology](/wiki/Environmental_physiology "Environmental physiology"), comparative physiology considers the diversity of functional characteristics across organisms.[[13]](#cite_note-Garland-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

### The classical era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The classical era")]

The study of human physiology as a medical field originates in [classical Greece](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece"), at the time of [Hippocrates](/wiki/Hippocrates "Hippocrates") (late 5th century BC).[[14]](#cite_note-14) Outside of Western tradition, early forms of physiology or anatomy can be reconstructed as having been present at around the same time in [China](/wiki/China "China"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) India[[16]](#cite_note-16) and elsewhere. Hippocrates incorporated the theory of [humorism](/wiki/Humorism "Humorism"), which consisted of four basic substances: earth, water, air and fire. Each substance is known for having a corresponding humor: black bile, phlegm, blood, and yellow bile, respectively. Hippocrates also noted some emotional connections to the four humors, on which [Galen](/wiki/Galen "Galen") would later expand. The critical thinking of [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") and his emphasis on the relationship between structure and function marked the beginning of physiology in [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"). Like [Hippocrates](/wiki/Hippocrates "Hippocrates"), Aristotle took to the humoral theory of disease, which also consisted of four primary qualities in life: hot, cold, wet and dry.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Galen (c. 130–200 AD) was the first to use experiments to probe the functions of the body. Unlike Hippocrates, Galen argued that humoral imbalances can be located in specific organs, including the entire body.[[18]](#cite_note-britannica.com-18) Galen also played off of Hippocrates' idea that emotions were also tied to the humors, and added the notion of temperaments: sanguine corresponds with blood; phlegmatic is tied to phlegm; yellow bile is connected to choleric; and black bile corresponds with melancholy. Galen also saw the human body consisting of three connected systems: the brain and nerves, which are responsible for thoughts and sensations; the heart and arteries, which give life; and the liver and veins, which can be attributed to nutrition and growth.[[18]](#cite_note-britannica.com-18) Galen was also the founder of experimental physiology.[[19]](#cite_note-19) And for the next 1,400 years, Galenic physiology was a powerful and influential tool in [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine").[[18]](#cite_note-britannica.com-18)

### Early modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Early modern period")]

[Jean Fernel](/wiki/Jean_Fernel "Jean Fernel") (1497–1558), a French physician, introduced the term "physiology".[[20]](#cite_note-20) Galen, [Ibn al-Nafis](/wiki/Ibn_al-Nafis "Ibn al-Nafis"), [Michael Servetus](/wiki/Michael_Servetus "Michael Servetus"), [Realdo Colombo](/wiki/Realdo_Colombo "Realdo Colombo"), [Amato Lusitano](/wiki/Amato_Lusitano "Amato Lusitano") and [William Harvey](/wiki/William_Harvey "William Harvey"), are credited as making important discoveries in the [circulation of the blood](/wiki/Circulation_of_the_blood "Circulation of the blood").[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Santorio Santorio](/wiki/Santorio_Santorio "Santorio Santorio") in 1610s was the first to use a device to measure the [pulse](/wiki/Pulse "Pulse") rate (the *pulsilogium*), and a [thermoscope](/wiki/Thermoscope "Thermoscope") to measure temperature.[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22)

In 1791 [Luigi Galvani](/wiki/Luigi_Galvani "Luigi Galvani") described the role of electricity in the nerves of dissected frogs. In 1811, [César Julien Jean Legallois](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Julien_Jean_Legallois "César Julien Jean Legallois") studied respiration in animal dissection and lesions and found the center of respiration in the [medulla oblongata](/wiki/Medulla_oblongata "Medulla oblongata"). In the same year, [Charles Bell](/wiki/Charles_Bell "Charles Bell") finished work on what would later become known as the [Bell–Magendie law](/wiki/Bell%E2%80%93Magendie_law "Bell–Magendie law"), which compared functional differences between dorsal and ventral roots of the [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord"). In 1824, [François Magendie](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Magendie "François Magendie") described the sensory roots and produced the first evidence of the cerebellum's role in [equilibration](/wiki/Equilibrioception "Equilibrioception") to complete the Bell–Magendie law.

In the 1820s, the French physiologist [Henri Milne-Edwards](/wiki/Henri_Milne-Edwards "Henri Milne-Edwards") introduced the notion of physiological division of labor, which allowed to "compare and study living things as if they were machines created by the industry of man." Inspired in the work of [Adam Smith](/wiki/Adam_Smith "Adam Smith"), Milne-Edwards wrote that the "body of all living beings, whether animal or plant, resembles a factory ... where the organs, comparable to workers, work incessantly to produce the phenomena that constitute the life of the individual." In more differentiated organisms, the functional labor could be apportioned between different instruments or [systems](/wiki/Biological_system "Biological system") (called by him as *appareils*).[[23]](#cite_note-brain-23)

In 1858, [Joseph Lister](/wiki/Joseph_Lister,_1st_Baron_Lister "Joseph Lister, 1st Baron Lister") studied the cause of blood coagulation and inflammation that resulted after previous injuries and surgical wounds. He later discovered and implemented [antiseptics](/wiki/Antiseptic "Antiseptic") in the operating room, and as a result, decreased the death rate from surgery by a substantial amount.[[24]](#cite_note-physiologyinfo.org-24)

[The Physiological Society](/wiki/The_Physiological_Society "The Physiological Society") was founded in London in 1876 as a dining club.[[25]](#cite_note-25) [The American Physiological Society](/wiki/The_American_Physiological_Society "The American Physiological Society") (APS) is a nonprofit organization that was founded in 1887. The Society is, "devoted to fostering education, scientific research, and dissemination of information in the physiological sciences."[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 1891, [Ivan Pavlov](/wiki/Ivan_Pavlov "Ivan Pavlov") performed research on "conditional responses" that involved dogs' saliva production in response to a bell and visual stimuli.[[24]](#cite_note-physiologyinfo.org-24)

In the 19th century, physiological knowledge began to accumulate at a rapid rate, in particular with the 1838 appearance of the [Cell theory](/wiki/Cell_theory "Cell theory") of [Matthias Schleiden](/wiki/Matthias_Jakob_Schleiden "Matthias Jakob Schleiden") and [Theodor Schwann](/wiki/Theodor_Schwann "Theodor Schwann").[[27]](#cite_note-27) It radically stated that organisms are made up of units called cells. [Claude Bernard](/wiki/Claude_Bernard "Claude Bernard")'s (1813–1878) further discoveries ultimately led to his concept of *[milieu interieur](/wiki/Milieu_interieur "Milieu interieur")* (internal environment),[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) which would later be taken up and championed as "[homeostasis](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis")" by American physiologist [Walter B. Cannon](/wiki/Walter_B._Cannon "Walter B. Cannon") in 1929. By homeostasis, Cannon meant "the maintenance of steady states in the body and the physiological processes through which they are regulated."[[30]](#cite_note-30) In other words, the body's ability to regulate its internal environment. William Beaumont was the first American to utilize the practical application of physiology.

Nineteenth-century physiologists such as [Michael Foster](/wiki/Michael_Foster_(physiologist) "Michael Foster (physiologist)"), [Max Verworn](/wiki/Max_Verworn "Max Verworn"), and [Alfred Binet](/wiki/Alfred_Binet "Alfred Binet"), based on [Haeckel](/wiki/Haeckel "Haeckel")'s ideas, elaborated what came to be called "general physiology", a unified science of life based on the cell actions,[[23]](#cite_note-brain-23) later renamed in the 20th century as [cell biology](/wiki/Cell_biology "Cell biology").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Late modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Late modern period")]

In the 20th century, biologists became interested in how organisms other than human beings function, eventually spawning the fields of [comparative physiology](/wiki/Comparative_physiology "Comparative physiology") and [ecophysiology](/wiki/Ecophysiology "Ecophysiology").[[32]](#cite_note-32) Major figures in these fields include [Knut Schmidt-Nielsen](/wiki/Knut_Schmidt-Nielsen "Knut Schmidt-Nielsen") and [George Bartholomew](/wiki/George_Bartholomew_(biologist) "George Bartholomew (biologist)"). Most recently, [evolutionary physiology](/wiki/Evolutionary_physiology "Evolutionary physiology") has become a distinct subdiscipline.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 1920, [August Krogh](/wiki/August_Krogh "August Krogh") won the Nobel Prize for discovering how, in capillaries, blood flow is regulated.[[24]](#cite_note-physiologyinfo.org-24)

In 1954, [Andrew Huxley](/wiki/Andrew_Huxley "Andrew Huxley") and Hugh Huxley, alongside their research team, discovered the sliding filaments in [skeletal muscle](/wiki/Skeletal_muscle "Skeletal muscle"), known today as the sliding filament theory.[[24]](#cite_note-physiologyinfo.org-24)

Recently, there have been intense debates about the vitality of physiology as a discipline (Is it dead or alive?).[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-:2-35) If physiology is perhaps less visible nowadays than during the golden age of the 19th century,[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36) it is in large part because the field has given birth to some of the most active domains of today's biological sciences, such as [neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience"), [endocrinology](/wiki/Endocrinology "Endocrinology"), and [immunology](/wiki/Immunology "Immunology").[[37]](#cite_note-37) Furthermore, physiology is still often seen as an integrative discipline, which can put together into a coherent framework data coming from various different domains.[[35]](#cite_note-:2-35)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Women in physiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Physiology&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Women in physiology")]

Initially, women were largely excluded from official involvement in any physiological society. The [American Physiological Society](/wiki/American_Physiological_Society "American Physiological Society"), for example, was founded in 1887 and included only men in its ranks.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In 1902, the American Physiological Society elected [Ida Hyde](/wiki/Ida_Hyde "Ida Hyde") as the first female member of the society.[[41]](#cite_note-Tucker_1981-41) Hyde, a representative of the [American Association of University Women](/wiki/American_Association_of_University_Women "American Association of University Women"), a global non-profit organization that advances equity for women and girls in education,[[42]](#cite_note-42) attempted to promote gender equality in every aspect of science and medicine.

Soon thereafter, in 1913, [J.S. Haldane](/wiki/J.S._Haldane "J.S. Haldane") proposed that women be allowed to formally join [The Physiological Society](/wiki/The_Physiological_Society "The Physiological Society"), which had been founded in 1876.[[43]](#cite_note-43) On 3 July 1915, six women were officially admitted: [Florence Buchanan](/wiki/Florence_Buchanan "Florence Buchanan"), [Winifred Cullis](/wiki/Winifred_Cullis "Winifred Cullis"), [Ruth Skelton](/wiki/Ruth_Skelton "Ruth Skelton"), [Sarah C. M. Sowton](/wiki/Sarah_C._M._Sowton "Sarah C. M. Sowton"), [Constance Leetham Terry](/wiki/Constance_Leetham "Constance Leetham"), and [Enid M. Tribe](/wiki/Enid_Tribe_Oppenheimer "Enid Tribe Oppenheimer").[[44]](#cite_note-physoc.org-44) The centenary of the election of women was celebrated in 2015 with the publication of the book "Women Physiologists: Centenary Celebrations And Beyond For The Physiological Society." ([ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-9933410-0-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-9933410-0-7 "Special:BookSources/978-0-9933410-0-7"))

Prominent women physiologists include:

* [Bodil Schmidt-Nielsen](/wiki/Bodil_Schmidt-Nielsen "Bodil Schmidt-Nielsen"), the first woman president of the [American Physiological Society](/wiki/American_Physiological_Society "American Physiological Society") in 1975.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* [Gerty Cori](/wiki/Gerty_Cori "Gerty Cori"),[[46]](#cite_note-46) along with her husband [Carl Cori](/wiki/Carl_Cori "Carl Cori"), received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1947 for their discovery of the [phosphate](/wiki/Phosphate "Phosphate")-containing form of [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose") known as [glycogen](/wiki/Glycogen "Glycogen"), as well as its function within [eukaryotic](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") [metabolic](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") mechanisms for energy production. Moreover, they discovered the [Cori cycle](/wiki/Cori_cycle "Cori cycle"), also known as the Lactic acid cycle,[[47]](#cite_note-47) which describes how muscle tissue converts glycogen into lactic acid via [lactic acid fermentation](/wiki/Lactic_acid_fermentation "Lactic acid fermentation").
* [Barbara McClintock](/wiki/Barbara_McClintock "Barbara McClintock") was rewarded the 1983 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for the discovery of [genetic transposition](/wiki/Transposition_(horizontal_gene_transfer) "Transposition (horizontal gene transfer)"). McClintock is the only female recipient who has won an unshared Nobel Prize.[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* [Gertrude Elion](/wiki/Gertrude_Elion "Gertrude Elion"),[[49]](#cite_note-49) along with [George Hitchings](/wiki/George_Hitchings "George Hitchings") and [Sir James Black](/wiki/James_Black_(pharmacologist) "James Black (pharmacologist)"), received the Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine in 1988 for their development of drugs employed in the treatment of several major diseases, such as [leukemia](/wiki/Leukemia "Leukemia"), some [autoimmune disorders](/wiki/Autoimmune_disorders "Autoimmune disorders"), [gout](/wiki/Gout "Gout"), [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria"), and [viral herpes](/wiki/Herpes "Herpes").
* [Linda B. Buck](/wiki/Linda_B._Buck "Linda B. Buck"),[[50]](#cite_note-nobelprize.org-50) along with [Richard Axel](/wiki/Richard_Axel "Richard Axel"), received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2004 for their discovery of [odorant receptors](/wiki/Olfactory_receptor "Olfactory receptor") and the complex organization of the [olfactory system](/wiki/Olfactory_system "Olfactory system").
* [Françoise Barré-Sinoussi](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7oise_Barr%C3%A9-Sinoussi "Françoise Barré-Sinoussi"),[[51]](#cite_note-51) along with [Luc Montagnier](/wiki/Luc_Montagnier "Luc Montagnier"), received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2008 for their work on the identification of the [Human Immunodeficiency Virus](/wiki/Human_Immunodeficiency_Virus "Human Immunodeficiency Virus") (HIV), the cause of [Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome](/wiki/Acquired_Immunodeficiency_Syndrome "Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome") (AIDS).
* [Elizabeth Blackburn](/wiki/Elizabeth_Blackburn "Elizabeth Blackburn"),[[52]](#cite_note-52) along with [Carol W. Greider](/wiki/Carol_W._Greider "Carol W. Greider")[[53]](#cite_note-53) and [Jack W. Szostak](/wiki/Jack_W._Szostak "Jack W. Szostak"), was awarded the 2009 Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine for the discovery of the genetic composition and function of [telomeres](/wiki/Telomeres "Telomeres") and the enzyme called [telomerase](/wiki/Telomerase "Telomerase").
